title: AI coding trends: 75% of enterprise teams to use AI IDEs by 2026
description: Industry analysis predicts 75% of enterprise development teams will use AI IDEs for full 'requirement-to-deployment' workflows by end of 2026. The global generative AI programming assistant market expected to reach $97.9 million by 2030. ByteDance's Trae, Cursor, and GLM-4.5 emerge as developer favorites.
author: Industry analysts

title: Claude code surpasses Codex in Google Trends
description: Google Trends data from February 2026 shows Claude code-related searches surpassing Codex, signaling a shift in the AI programming tools market. This reflects Anthropic's growing influence in AI-assisted programming.
author: Market analysis

title: AI reasoning models learn when to 'stop thinking'
description: Beihang University, ByteDance, and Renmin University published arXiv:2602.08354v1, discovering that AI reasoning models can learn when to stop thinking. Accepted to ICML 2026, this addresses computational efficiency in reasoning models.
author: Beihang University, ByteDance, Renmin University

title: Ladybird adopts Rust, with help from AI
description: Andreas Kling shared a case study on sophisticated use of coding agents for ambitious projects. Ladybird switched from Swift to Rust, using Claude Code and Codex to port their LibJS JavaScript engine (25,000 lines of Rust in two weeks instead of months). Key insight: Having existing conformance testing suites (like test262) makes agentic engineering much safer, as you can compare output with trusted implementations.
author: Simon Willison (citing Andreas Kling)

title: Writing about Agentic Engineering Patterns
description: Simon Willison launched a new project to document Agentic Engineering Patterns—coding practices and patterns for getting the best results from coding agent development. This reflects the growing maturity of AI-assisted programming as a discipline.
author: Simon Willison

title: Writing code is cheap now
description: The biggest challenge in adopting agentic engineering is getting comfortable with the fact that writing code is now cheap. This fundamental shift challenges decades of engineering habits built around code being expensive. Planning and design remain crucial, but the cost of implementation has dropped dramatically.
author: Simon Willison

title: Red/green TDD with coding agents
description: A key agentic engineering pattern: Use red/green TDD (Test Driven Development) to get better results from coding agents. Writing tests first and having the agent iterate until tests pass leads to more reliable outcomes.
author: Simon Willison

title: The Claude C Compiler: What It Reveals About the Future of Software
description: Chris Lattner's review of Anthropic's Claude C Compiler (built with parallel Claudes). Key insights: Good software depends on judgment, communication, and clear abstraction—AI has amplified this. AI coding automates implementation, making design and stewardship more important. CCC looks like a competent textbook implementation but isn't production-ready yet.
author: Simon Willison (citing Chris Lattner)

title: How I think about Codex
description: Gabriel Chua (OpenAI) explains that Codex = Model + Harness + Surfaces. Notably, Codex models are trained in the presence of the harness—tool use, execution loops, and iterative verification are part of how the model learns, not bolted-on behaviors.
author: Simon Willison (citing Gabriel Chua)

title: OpenClaw deployment on Alibaba Cloud with Qwen integration
description: Alibaba Cloud released quick deployment guides and 'Coding Plan' packages optimized for OpenClaw, integrating with Qwen (Tongyi Qianwen) models for Chinese users. This marks the first major cloud provider to officially support OpenClaw deployment.
author: Alibaba Cloud

title: Andrej Karpathy talks about 'Claws'
description: Andrej Karpathy tweeted about buying a Mac Mini to tinker with Claws. He views Claws as a new layer on top of LLM agents, taking orchestration, scheduling, context, tool calls, and persistence to the next level. NanoClaw is highlighted as interesting (~4,000 lines of code, container-based). The term 'Claw' is becoming a category name for OpenClaw-like agent systems that run on personal hardware.
author: Simon Willison (citing Andrej Karpathy)

title: Taalas serves Llama 3.1 8B at 17,000 tokens/second
description: Canadian hardware startup Taalas announced custom hardware running Llama 3.1 8B at 17,000 tokens/second. Their 'Silicon Llama' uses aggressive quantization (3-bit and 6-bit parameters). Demo available at chatjimmy.ai.
author: Simon Willison

title: ggml.ai joins Hugging Face
description: Georgi Gerganov's ggml.ai (creator of llama.cpp) is joining Hugging Face. This is significant because Georgi's work in March 2023 made it possible to run local LLMs on consumer hardware, kicking off the local model movement. The collaboration aims for seamless integration between transformers and ggml ecosystems, and better packaging/user experience for local model software.
author: Simon Willison

title: Prompt caching enables long-running agentic products
description: Thariq Shihipar from Anthropic explains that prompt caching makes products like Claude Code feasible by reusing computation and decreasing latency/cost. Claude Code's entire harness is built around prompt caching, with high cache hit rates enabling more generous rate limits.
author: Simon Willison (citing Thariq Shihipar)

title: Lex Fridman interviews Peter Steinberger about OpenClaw
description: Lex Fridman conducted a deep interview with Peter Steinberger in February 2026, covering OpenClaw's development, the 'mom test' for AI accessibility, and Steinberger's philosophy that 'You can't compete with someone who's doing it purely for fun.' The interview was noted as Steinberger's last before joining OpenAI.
author: Lex Fridman Podcast

title: Gemini 3.1 Pro released
description: Google released Gemini 3.1 Pro, priced at $2/million input and $12/million output (less than half of Claude Opus 4.6). Notable features include improved SVG animation performance. However, the model experienced slow response times and errors at launch due to high demand. Simon tested the model's ability to generate SVG illustrations of pelicans riding bicycles.
author: Simon Willison

title: SWE-bench February 2026 leaderboard update
description: SWE-bench released fresh benchmark results for current models. Top performers: Claude 4.5 Opus (76.8%), Gemini 3 Flash (75.8%), MiniMax M2.5 (75.8%), Claude Opus 4.6 (75.6%). Notable: Claude 4.5 beats 4.6, several Chinese models (GLM-5, Kimi K2.5, DeepSeek V3.2) in top 10. OpenAI's GPT-5.2 at position 6, with GPT-5.3-Codex not represented.
author: Simon Willison

title: GitHub trending: TypeScript and AI Agent dominance in 2026
description: GitHub trending analysis for early 2026 shows TypeScript as the biggest winner with AI Agent projects experiencing comprehensive breakout. OpenClaw, Cursor, and various AI coding tools dominated monthly trending lists, signaling a fundamental shift in developer workflows.
author: GitHub community analysis

title: OpenClaw: The fastest-growing open source project in history
description: OpenClaw achieved 175,000-210,000+ GitHub stars in weeks, becoming the fastest-growing open source project. It's a local-first AI agent platform running 24/7 on personal hardware, integrating with WeChat, Telegram, Slack, and Discord. Features include persistent memory, proactive execution, and natural language-driven automation.
author: Community (Hacker News, GitHub trending)

title: Microsoft Research: AI agents achieve detective-style exploration
description: Microsoft Research and KAIST published arXiv:2602.23008v1, showing AI agents achieving 'detective-style exploration and experience internalization.' Accepted to ICLR 2026, focusing on agent learning and exploration strategies.
author: Microsoft Research, KAIST

title: 2026 AI coding tool landscape: 85% adoption rate
description: Comprehensive reviews of AI coding tools in February 2026 show 85% of developers now regularly use AI coding tools. Top tools include Cursor ($20/month), Windsurf ($15/month), GitHub Copilot ($10/month), and Trae (free from ByteDance). Evolution shows progression from chat-based to integrated workflow tools.
author: Tech community (Juejin, Toutiao)

title: OpenClaw founder Peter Steinberger joins OpenAI
description: Peter Steinberger, creator of OpenClaw (formerly Moltbot/Clawdbot), joined OpenAI on February 16, 2026, to lead next-generation personal agent development. OpenClaw became the fastest-growing GitHub project in history with 175,000+ stars. The project will transition to an independent non-profit foundation funded by OpenAI, keeping the code open source.
author: Multiple sources (via Lex Fridman interview coverage)

title: MIT Technology Review: Generative coding enters mature stage
description: MIT Technology Review's '10 Breakthrough Technologies 2026' highlights generative coding maturity. AI now writes 30% of Microsoft's code and 25%+ of Google's code. GitHub Copilot and similar tools enable users with weak programming skills to create applications through detailed prompts.
author: MIT Technology Review

title: Code2World: AI learns to predict future interface changes
description: USTC and Oxford University researchers published Code2World (arXiv:2602.09856v1), enabling AI to predict and automate phone/computer operations like humans. The breakthrough allows AI to anticipate interface changes and execute tasks automatically.
author: USTC, Oxford University, Alibaba Group

title: Paper2Code: Multi-agent system converts ML papers to code
description: Paper2Code/PaperCoder is an open-source multi-agent LLM system that automatically converts machine learning research papers into executable code repositories. It addresses the reproducibility crisis (only 21% of ML papers share code) with a three-stage approach: Planning, Analysis, and Code Generation. Achieved 77% approval from original authors and supports DeepSeek API.
author: Research community

title: ICLR 2026 controversy: 21% of paper reviews written by AI
description: Reddit's r/MachineLearning community discussed controversy where 21% of ICLR 2026 paper reviews were allegedly written by AI. Users expressed concerns about review quality and the implications of AI-assisted peer review.
author: r/MachineLearning community

title: DeepSeek mHC architecture sparks Reddit discussions
description: DeepSeek's new mHC (Manifold-Constrained Hyper-connection) architecture heated up r/MachineLearning discussions in February 2026. Called '2026开年大礼包' (2026 New Year's Big Gift), the architecture could potentially disrupt Transformer models with hyper-connections.
author: r/MachineLearning community
